By Molly Mary O’Brien Pop/R&B F.I.G Naomi Scott With elegant, modern takes on 1980s soft ballads and sophistipop, the former Disney Channel actor’s debut LP is as stylish as it is easygoing. By Kiana Mickles Rap Body of Work Edward Skeletrix Conceptual mastermind or serial con man? On his latest album of shaky rapping and abstract interludes, the multi-hyphenate creator finds a strange middle ground. By Olivier Lafontant Pop/R&B Easy EP Smerz The Norwegian duo’s latest EP, inspired by the final track of its 2025 album, forgoes straightforward plots for stranger, smudgier snapshots of life on the precipice of adulthood. By Hattie Lindert Rock Little Wide Open Kevin Morby Working with Aaron Dessner, the singer-songwriter balances contentment and mortal anxiety on the most cohesive and tuneful album of his career. By Will Hermes See More More Reviews Experimental Tranquilizer Oneohtrix Point Never Best New Album Drawing on a cache of commercial sample CDs, Daniel Lopatin assembles an impossibly dense and transportive electronic album that takes impermanence as its inspiration. Pop/R&B Addison Addison Rae The girlypop album of summer warrants comparison to Lana, Madonna, and most of all, Britney. Are we ready for Addison? Rock Snocaps Snocaps The Crutchfield twins—accompanied by MJ Lenderman and Brad Cook—reunite for a spirited new project that plays to their strengths and embraces all the miles they’ve traveled. Pop/R&B LUX Rosalía Best New Album The Spanish singer’s fourth record is a heartfelt offering of avant-garde classical pop that roars through genre, romance, and religion. Rap Stardust Danny Brown Working with a new vanguard of outsider electronic artists, the Detroit rapper lets his freak flag fry. The results are shaky but full of heart. Pop/R&B West End Girl Lily Allen With an album that doubles as an insider’s account of a tabloid divorce, the singer finds a new evolution of her signature style: Lightness isn’t a foil for irony, but a vehicle for hurt. Rap Lil Herb G Herbo Stepping into his teenage shoes for a loose concept album, the Chicago icon reflects on the original era of drill with the clarity of a scene veteran. Electronic Repulsor Shlohmo The L.A. beatmaker turns aggressive on his fourth album—dialing up the distortion, flooding his beats with overdriven synths, and pushing anxious moods into the red. Today, we look back at Santana’s transitional fourth album, a transcendental convergence of rock, psych, and Afro-Cuban styles that absolutely rips. Rock Fanny Hill Fanny Each Sunday, Pitchfork takes an in-depth look at a significant album from the past, and any record not in our archives is eligible. Today, we look back at the 1972 album from Fanny, a real rock spectacle laced with tenderness, sisterhood, and impeccable riffs that just never got its due. Pop/R&B Book of Love Book of Love Each Sunday, Pitchfork takes an in-depth look at a significant album from the past, and any record not in our archives is eligible. Today, we look back at the beguiling debut album from a quartet of art-school students who brought a slyly subversive touch to club-friendly new wave. Pop/R&B Life for Rent Dido Each Sunday, Pitchfork takes an in-depth look at a significant album from the past, and any record not in our archives is eligible. Today, we revisit the UK singer’s second album, a fascinatingly normal early-aughts pop record from a woman whose still waters run deep. Folk/Country / Rock The Changer and the Changed Cris Williamson Each Sunday, Pitchfork takes an in-depth look at a significant album from the past, and any record not in our archives is eligible. Today, we revisit the spare, gentle, semi-forgotten singer-songwriter album that’s fueled 50 years of “sapphic pop.” Rock Generation X Generation X Each Sunday, Pitchfork takes an in-depth look at a significant album from the past, and any record not in our archives is eligible. Today, we look back at Billy Idol’s first band, a combustible 1978 record with a punk sound and pop politics. Pop/R&B Full Moon Brandy Each Sunday, Pitchfork takes an in-depth look at a significant album from the past, and any record not in our archives is eligible. Today, we look back at Brandy’s futuristic, full-hearted 2002 record, an album so lush and expertly crafted that it became the new blueprint for the pop-R&B sound. Jazz / Pop/R&B Straight From the Heart Patrice Rushen Each Sunday, Pitchfork takes an in-depth look at a significant album from the past, and any record not in our archives is eligible. Today, we look back at the 1982 breakthrough from a California pianist and songwriter whose effortless fusion of R&B, disco, and jazz defied any attempts to pin her down. Angry Metal Guy - Metal Reviews, Interviews and General Angryness Blog Reviews Interviews About Contact FAQ Blog Reviews Interviews About Contact FAQ Arroganz – Death Doom Punks Review By Andy-War-Hall on May 15, 2026 in Reviews , Death Metal , Doom Metal , Punk , 0 comments “Once, punk ruled my heart as jealously as metal. I loved the heart-in-hand abandon and DIY ethos of acts like Rise Against , Social Distortion , Bad Religion , and Descendants , and though I still do metal nonetheless won out as my root genre. But punk and metal have always overlapped, and when the two offshoots of rock tango together, great things can happen. German death metallers Arroganz have been carrying out this uneasy marriage since 2008, dropping six testaments of metallic brutality and punkish rebellion over the years.” Skin(head) to win. Restless Spirit – Restless Spirit Review By Steel Druhm on May 14, 2026 in Reviews , Doom Metal , Stoner Rock , 16 comments “When one thinks of Long Island, stoner doom may not be the first musical variant that comes to mind. Restless Spirit have been out to change that since 2015, dropping several EPs and 3 long players of massive, weighty music with toes in the pools frequented by Mastodon , C.O.C. , and The Sword . 2023s Afterimage was a great album crushed beneath a disastrous production that made enjoyment nigh impossible. Now comes their self-titled 4th album and a bit of a course correction.” Spirits a drifting. Crown Lands – Apocalypse Review By Owlswald on May 14, 2026 in Reviews , Progressive Rock , Rock , 79 comments “ Rush is my all-time favorite band, so their 2015 hiatus, brought on by Neil Peart’s declining health hit me hard. Draconian – In Somnolent Ruin Review By Kenstrosity on May 13, 2026 in Reviews , Death Doom , Death Metal , Doom Metal , Gothic Metal , 75 comments “ Draconian is an institution.
